mission.title: Install and Configure Container2wasm on Kubernetes
mission.description: container2wasm is a tool that converts container images into WebAssembly (WASM) format, enabling them to run on WASI runtimes or in the browser. Installing this tool allows you to leverage existing containerized applications in a lightweight, portable format suitable for various environments.

steps.title: Create a Namespace
steps.description: First, create a dedicated namespace for the container2wasm installation.

steps.title: Apply the Deployment Manifest
steps.description: Next, apply the deployment manifest to install container2wasm.

steps.title: Apply the Service Manifest
steps.description: Then, apply the service manifest to expose the container2wasm application.

steps.title: Verify the Installation
steps.description: Finally, verify that the pods are running correctly.

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the container2wasm pods running in the 'container2wasm' namespace, and you will be able to convert container images to WASM format. You can verify the functionality by executing the conversion command and checking for the output file.

uninstall.title: Uninstall container2wasm
uninstall.description: To uninstall container2wasm, delete the deployment and service, and then remove the namespace.

upgrade.title: Upgrade container2wasm
upgrade.description: To upgrade container2wasm, apply the new version of the manifests.

troubleshooting.title: Pod Not Starting
troubleshooting.description: If the pod is not starting, check the pod logs for errors using: `kubectl logs <pod-name> -n container2wasm`.

troubleshooting.title: Service Not Accessible
troubleshooting.description: If the service is not accessible, ensure that the correct port is exposed in the service manifest.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient Resources
troubleshooting.description: If you encounter resource issues, check the resource requests and limits in the deployment manifest and adjust them as necessary.

prerequisites.description: Ensure that you have a running Kubernetes cluster and kubectl installed and configured to communicate with your cluster.
